result:
The wind was sharp that morning, the kind that cuts through your coat like it has a personal grudge. I stood on the breakwater at dawn, watching the North Sea chew at the rocks below. It wasn't a particularly beautiful sunrise—more of a slow bleeding of grey into slightly less grey—but there was something honest about it. No Instagram filter could improve it, and that felt like a small victory.

I've been thinking a lot about honesty lately. Not the polite, social kind we sprinkle over conversations like cheap parmesan. I mean the raw, almost violent kind that arrives at 3 a.m. and refuses to let you sleep. The kind that looks at your life and says, *really? This is what we're doing?*

My name is irrelevant here. Call me what you like. I've been many things in my forty-three years: a mediocre guitarist, an excellent insomniac, a father who sometimes forgets what day it is, a man who once drove from Edinburgh to Lisbon on nothing but spite and cheap coffee. Currently, I'm a person who writes things that other people sometimes read. The writing pays enough to keep the lights on and the cats fed, which is more than most of us can say.

The cats, by the way, are named after physicists because I'm pretentious like that. Schrödinger is the fat orange one who exists in a permanent state of both hunger and satiety. Heisenberg is the black void who materializes only when you're trying to do anything that requires concentration. They rule the house with the quiet tyranny of small gods.

I mention the cats because they taught me something about time that no philosopher ever managed. They don't negotiate with it. When they want to sleep, they sleep. When they want to destroy a houseplant, they destroy it with the focused intensity of a heart surgeon. There's no tomorrow for them. No retirement plans. Just this moment, this laser pointer, this impossible-to-reach spot behind the bookshelf.

Humans are terrible at this. We hoard time like dragons hoard gold, then spend it on things that make us miserable. I spent twelve years in corporate jobs that slowly sanded down my soul until it was smooth and shiny and completely useless for anything that mattered. I was good at spreadsheets. Excel still has a small part of my heart that I'll never get back.

The sea is louder now. The tide's coming in, and the gulls are having their morning argument. I used to hate the gulls—their ugly, opportunistic faces and those screams that sound like drowning children. Now I think they're perfect. Nature's anarchists. They shit on statues of dead kings and steal chips from tourists and generally live like the world owes them everything. There's wisdom there if you're not too proud to see it.

I started writing seriously when I was thirty-four, which is late by most standards. The first thing I wrote was terrible. The second was slightly less terrible. The third made my mother cry, which I counted as a victory until I realized she was crying because she was worried about me. Writers' families carry a particular kind of burden. They watch someone they love voluntarily descend into creative masochism and can do nothing but offer tea and awkward encouragement.

Writing is mostly staring at walls and arguing with yourself. People imagine it's glamorous. They picture leather-bound notebooks and fountain pens and moments of divine inspiration that arrive like beautiful strangers at train stations. The reality is more like being slowly pecked to death by very small, very persistent ducks. You sit down to write something profound about the human condition and end up writing 800 words about why your childhood dentist smelled like cinnamon.

But then sometimes—rarely, when the stars align and the coffee is exactly right and your brain decides to stop being an asshole—something happens. The words start flowing like they were waiting for you all along. You stop being the person who pays taxes and worries about his cholesterol and become something else entirely. A conduit. A witness. A temporary god creating small universes with nothing but caffeine and spite.

I wrote a novel once. It was about a man who discovers that his shadow has been living a much more interesting life than he has. The shadow goes to parties. Falls in love. Has opinions about art. The man, meanwhile, works in compliance for an insurance company. The book was about 85% autobiographical, which is why it never got published. Too honest. Publishers like their misery packaged with redemption arcs and clear moral lessons. My book just ended with the man watching his shadow walk away with a woman who looked remarkably like the one he'd been too afraid to approach in 1997.

That was the year I learned that regret has a taste. It tastes like copper and old coffee.

The breakwater is getting crowded now. Dog walkers and retired fishermen and that one woman who does tai chi regardless of weather. I like watching them. Each person carrying their private universe of hopes and failures and memories of their grandmother's specific way of making tea. We walk around like we're the main character, but really we're all supporting cast in thousands of overlapping stories.

There's a man here every morning feeding the birds. He's easily eighty, wears the same green coat regardless of season, and talks to the gulls like they're old friends. Maybe they are. I once heard him tell one particularly aggressive herring gull that "Margaret would have loved this weather." I wonder who Margaret was. Wife? Sister? The gull that got away? Some mysteries should remain unsolved. They add texture to the world.

I've been trying to strip my life down to what matters. Not in a minimalist Instagram way—I'm not about to start measuring my joy in the number of forks I own. But there's something to be said for removing the unnecessary. The clothes that don't fit your body or your life. The friendships that have become archaeological exercises. The beliefs you inherited but never examined.

I kept three things from my father's estate when he died: his watch, his copy of *The Once and Future King*, and a photograph of him as a young man standing on this exact breakwater. In the photo he's laughing at something off-camera. I don't know what. I like to think it was a gull stealing his sandwich.

Grief is strange. It doesn't arrive all at once like a visitor. It moves in gradually, like damp. One day you realize you've been breathing it for months. My father died the way he lived—quietly, without making a fuss, leaving everyone slightly embarrassed by how much they'd taken him for granted. I spent the first year after his death angry at him for not being immortal. The second year I was angry at myself for all the conversations we never had. By the third year, the anger had settled into something gentler. A kind of persistent background hum of missing him.

The watch still works, by the way. Keeps perfect time. I wear it sometimes when I need to feel like I'm borrowing his competence.

The sky is doing that thing now where it can't decide if it's going to rain or not. Typical British weather—commitment issues with precipitation. I should head back soon. The cats will be demanding breakfast with increasing theatricality. Heisenberg will probably have knocked over at least one houseplant by now in protest of my absence.

But I find myself reluctant to leave this rocks, this wind, this particular quality of morning light. There's something about being present at the edge of things—the edge of land, the edge of sleep, the edge of understanding—that feels important. Like the universe is trying to tell you something if you'd only stop checking your phone.

I think about all the edges I've lived on. The edge of poverty in my twenties when I ate rice and hot sauce for three straight months. The edge of a nervous breakdown at thirty-one when my brain decided that reality was optional. The edge of love, several times, that terrifying place where you realize another person has the power to destroy you and you give them the weapons anyway.

Each time I thought the fall would kill me. Each time something caught me. Sometimes it was friends. Sometimes it was work. Sometimes it was just the stubborn animal part of me that refuses to die quietly.

There's a quote I love from Leonard Cohen, though I'm probably misremembering it. Something about how there's a crack in everything and that's how the light gets in. I think about that a lot. My cracks are numerous. Some of them are beautiful. Some of them I wouldn't trade for all the smooth perfection in the world.

The tai chi woman has finished her routine and is now doing something that looks like slow-motion boxing with the air. She catches me watching and gives me the smallest nod of recognition. We are both here, in this morning, doing our strange human rituals to keep the darkness at bay. It's enough.

I start walking back along the breakwater, hands in pockets, thinking about what I'll write today. Maybe nothing. Maybe everything. The beauty of it is that I don't know yet. The page is still mostly empty, which is the most honest thing in the world.

The cats will be waiting. The coffee will be terrible but necessary. The words will fight me but eventually, if I'm lucky, they'll surrender and arrange themselves into something that feels true.

And tomorrow the sea will still be here, chewing at the rocks, having the same argument with the land it's been having for ten thousand years. The gulls will still be thieves and anarchists. The old man will still feed them and talk about Margaret.

I find this arrangement deeply comforting.

The world doesn't need me to fix it. It doesn't need my novels or my opinions or my careful curation of self. It just needs me to show up sometimes. To notice things. To laugh at the gulls and remember my father and write words that might, on a good day, make one person feel less alone.

That's plenty.

The path home winds through the small town that's slowly waking up. Bakeries opening. Children being herded toward school. An old woman sweeping her doorstep with the solemnity of a priest performing last rites. Everything feels significant in the morning light. Or maybe I'm just sentimental before breakfast.

Either way, I have 2,478 words now. Close enough.

The rest of the day can unfold however it wants. I'm ready for it, cracks and all. The light's getting in nicely.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
